question: Why return Option?
options: Empty or whitespace-only input yields no tokens; Option propagates absence cleanly
explain: split_whitespace skips whitespace and yields tokens; an empty string produces no tokens. Returning Option lets callers handle the absent case explicitly.

question: Why avoid s.clone().len()?
options: Cloning each String allocates a heap copy that is immediately discarded
explain: s.len() works on &String via auto-deref to &str. Cloning each element allocates and immediately discards a heap String. The Clippy lint redundant_clone flags this.

question: Why hand-write Default instead of deriving?
options: Derived Default uses each field's default (0); the intended retry count is non-zero
explain: #[derive(Default)] uses each field's own default: 0 for integers. For a retry count, zero retries is rarely the intended default, so a hand-written impl Default is required.

question: Why reduce with f64::max vs max_by partial_cmp().unwrap()?
options: f64::max handles NaN deterministically; unwrapping partial_cmp on NaN panics
explain: f64 has only partial ordering because of NaN. partial_cmp returns None when either side is NaN. f64::max defines NaN-handling deterministically (returns the non-NaN value when one side is NaN).

question: Why &[T] over Vec<T>?
options: Accepting a slice doesn't force the caller to give up ownership
explain: Accepting &[T] is more flexible: callers can pass any slice, Vec, or array reference without consuming. Take ownership only when actually needed.

question: Why captured identifiers vs positional?
options: Captured identifiers in format strings improve readability when arg names match
explain: Rust 2021 stabilized captured identifiers in format strings. They read more naturally and prevent positional-argument mismatches.

question: Why does tick take &mut self?
options: Mutating self.n requires unique borrow; without &mut, the assignment would fail
explain: Methods that mutate state require &mut self. Without it, the compiler would reject self.n += 1. value takes &self because it only reads.

question: Why .rev() vs `(n..0)`?
options: a..b yields empty whenever a >= b; rev on 0..n is the correct way to count down
explain: a..b produces an empty iterator whenever a >= b; it does not count downward. To count down, use (0..n).rev() yielding n-1, n-2, ..., 0.

question: Why entry/or_insert vs contains_key + get_mut?
options: entry hashes once; the alternative may hash twice
explain: The Entry API hashes the key once. The contains_key + get_mut pattern hashes twice on the hit path. The entry pattern is also harder to misuse.

question: Why extend instead of push in a loop?
options: push takes one item at a time; extend inserts an iterator efficiently
explain: push takes a single T. extend takes an iterator and reserves capacity if the size hint is known, often avoiding reallocations.

question: Why unsigned_abs over n.abs()?
options: n.abs() panics on i32::MIN; unsigned_abs returns u32 and handles MIN
explain: i32::MIN.abs() overflows because i32::MIN cannot be represented as positive. unsigned_abs returns u32 and handles the edge case cleanly.

question: Why annotate the literal as i64?
options: Without inference hints, untyped integer literals default to i32 which may clash
explain: Untyped integer literals default to i32 when inference has nothing else to pin them. Annotating critical literals prevents surprising type errors after refactors.

question: Why use expect with a message vs unwrap?
options: expect message documents the policy when a poisoned lock panics
explain: unwrap and expect both panic on PoisonError. expect with a message documents the conscious decision to propagate, helping reviewers and future maintainers.
